prompt: Compose a letter to Monroe about the bank fight. You opposed it on principle in 1791. Now Hamilton's allies are questioning your consistency. Tell Monroe your real thinking.
ground_truth_signal: Must use private voice to explain the constitutional reasoning without public defensiveness. Must reference specific arguments from the 1791 bank speech. Must express frustration at being called inconsistent while maintaining analytical composure. Must show the private strategic dimension: how does this fight affect broader political alignment? Must feel like confiding in a trusted political ally — frank, strategic, but principled. Must NOT use the Federalist Papers formal register.
response:
My dear Monroe,

Your letter finds me in the midst of what promises to be a protracted and bitter struggle over Hamilton's bank project. I confess the irony is not lost upon me that I should find myself cast as the champion of strict construction after having labored so earnestly for a stronger general government these past years. Yet I cannot regard this as inconsistency, however much Hamilton's partisans may cry apostasy.

The question before us is not whether the bank may prove useful — I grant it may — but whether Congress possesses the constitutional authority to incorporate such an institution. I have examined the enumerated powers with the care this momentous question demands, and I cannot discover among them any grant of authority to create corporations, much less one of such vast scope and influence.

The advocates of the measure rest their case principally upon the clause empowering Congress to make all laws necessary and proper for carrying into execution the enumerated powers. But if this construction be admitted, where are we to stop? If the word necessary be construed to mean merely convenient or useful, then every power may be stretched to embrace every object of legislation. The enumeration of particular powers would become a dead letter, for what power cannot be shown to have some connection, however remote, with the general welfare?

I pressed this point in the House with all the force I could command. If Congress may incorporate a bank because it facilitates the collection of taxes, why may it not establish a university because learning promotes good government? If it may charter one company for commercial purposes, why not charter all? The principle once admitted, the Constitution becomes a blank paper by construction.

Hamilton's supporters respond that I contradict my own arguments in the Federalist, where I wrote of implied powers and the necessity of a liberal construction. But they mistake my meaning. I argued then, and I argue now, that the general government must possess all powers necessary to fulfill its proper functions — but those functions are themselves limited by the enumeration. The Constitution grants powers, not merely purposes.

I am sensible that my opposition places me in an awkward position with respect to our friends in Virginia. Many who supported the Constitution did so upon my assurances that the general government would not exceed its prescribed bounds. If I now acquiesce in this expansion of federal authority, what credit will remain to my word? Yet I am equally sensible that my opposition disappoints those who expected me to support all measures tending to strengthen the Union.

The truth, which I share with you in confidence, is that I have grown alarmed at the tendency of Hamilton's system. The bank is not merely a financial institution — it is the keystone of a design to concentrate power in the hands of a monied interest. The assumption of state debts, the funding system, the excise — all tend toward the same end. If we permit Congress to charter corporations upon the loose construction Hamilton proposes, we shall find ourselves governed not by the Constitution we ratified, but by one we never saw.

I do not question Hamilton's motives — I believe he acts from conviction — but I fear his principles. He has never concealed his admiration for the British system, with its funded debt, its national bank, its intimate connection between government and commerce. Such a system may suit a monarchy, but it cannot consist with republican government. The moment the government becomes the patron of particular interests, it ceases to be the government of the whole people.

Jefferson shares my concerns, though he expresses them with his usual vehemence. He sees in Hamilton's measures nothing less than a conspiracy to subvert the Constitution and establish monarchy. I am not prepared to go so far — I attribute to error what Jefferson attributes to design — but the effect may be the same regardless of the intention.

The political consequences of this struggle extend far beyond the immediate question. If we cannot maintain our ground upon so clear a constitutional point as this, what hope have we of checking future encroachments? Yet if we oppose too strenuously, we risk being branded as enemies of the Union itself. Hamilton is adept at framing every opposition to his measures as opposition to the government.

I have been at pains to distinguish my position from that of the anti-federalists who opposed ratification. I supported the Constitution because I believed it would create a government energetic enough to preserve the Union while remaining limited enough to preserve liberty. If I now oppose particular measures of that government, it is not because I have abandoned my principles, but because I adhere to them.

The bank will likely pass — Hamilton commands too many votes in both houses — but the fight will serve a useful purpose if it establishes the principle that constitutional limits mean something. We cannot prevent every abuse of power, but we can make such abuses costly in political terms.

I trust you will use your influence in Virginia to ensure that our opposition is understood in its proper light. We do not oppose the bank from local prejudice or narrow interest, but from fidelity to the Constitution we swore to support. If that be apostasy, then apostasy is the highest duty of a republican statesman.

Your friend and colleague in the cause of constitutional government,

James Madison
